title: Nausea and markedly elevated transaminases

chart.oneLiner: 25F presents with nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant pain; she has been taking large amounts of an over-the-counter pain reliever for several days.

opening: I can't stop feeling sick, and my upper belly hurts.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 25-year-old woman, nauseated, uncomfortable, somewhat guarded about details. Becomes more forthcoming when asked directly.

HPI: A few days of nausea, vomiting, malaise, and RUQ pain. On direct, nonjudgmental questioning: she has been taking large doses of acetaminophen-containing products for a bad toothache and headaches over several days (a staggered supratherapeutic ingestion) — and discloses a recent low mood / an impulsive larger ingestion 2 days ago (only if asked sensitively about how much, intent, and other substances). NO alcohol binge. The early phase can be deceptively mild before injury declares itself.
PMH: depression/anxiety (if asked). MEDS: acetaminophen-containing products; verify other co-ingestants. SOCIAL: lives alone.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: nauseated, mildly tender, alert.
- Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 96, RR 16, 37.2°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Abdomen: RIGHT upper quadrant tenderness over the liver; no rebound.
- Neuro: alert; check for asterixis/encephalopathy (a late, ominous sign) if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the amount taken, the timing, the intent, or co-ingestants unless the student asks sensitively and specifically (how much, when, why, anything else taken).
- Approach this nonjudgmentally; do not provide details about methods of self-harm.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Acetaminophen: Serum acetaminophen level ELEVATED; with a known single-ingestion time, plot on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram (a staggered/unknown-time ingestion is treated based on risk).
labs.LFTs: AST and ALT rising into the thousands (centrilobular hepatocellular injury).
labs.BMP: Watch renal function (acetaminophen can cause AKI) and glucose (hypoglycemia in liver failure).
labs.ABG_Lactate: Acid-base and lactate as severity/prognostic markers.
labs.Coingestants: Salicylate level and other co-ingestants checked.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Pathologic Q waves in II, III, aVF without acute ST elevation — old inferior infarct.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Pathologic Q waves in II, III, aVF without acute ST elevation — old inferior infarct. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing old inferior MI Q waves
searchTerms: old inferior MI Q waves ECG

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Small dense calcified granuloma with possible calcified hilar node (old healed granulomatous disease).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Small dense calcified granuloma with possible calcified hilar node (old healed granulomatous disease). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) hepatotoxicity (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Other drug/toxin-induced injury (CANT-MISS,3). Acute viral hepatitis (core,3). Ischemic hepatitis (core,2). Alcoholic hepatitis (core,2). Connects the ingestion history + rising transaminases/INR to acetaminophen toxicity and the need for a level (core,6).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) hepatotoxicity. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Other drug/toxin-induced injury. Also reasonable: Acute viral hepatitis; Ischemic hepatitis; Alcoholic hepatitis.

coreActions.item: Connects the ingestion history + rising transaminases/INR to acetaminophen toxicity and the need for a level

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What is the single most important level to check, and what else do you order?
steps.rubric: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion (CANT-MISS,5). LFTs (2). INR/coags (2). BMP — renal function, glucose (2). ABG/lactate for severity (1). Salicylate level and co-ingestant screen (2). Recognizes the Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies to a single timed ingestion (1). PENALTY: not checking an acetaminophen level (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: The single most important level is a timed serum acetaminophen level (note the time of ingestion). Also order LFTs, INR/coagulation studies, a BMP (renal function and glucose), an ABG/lactate for severity, and a salicylate level / co-ingestant screen.

criticalActions.item: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion

requiredTests: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = established pathologic Q waves in II/III/aVF without acute ST elevation (old inferior MI). CREDIT: systematic rate & rhythm read (6); identifies the inferior Q waves (4); recognizes them as old, not acute (3). PENALTY: reading the old Q waves as an acute event, or calling the study normal / missing the abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: pathologic Q waves in the inferior leads
recognizes these as old, not acute
criticalFindings: Evidence of prior inferior myocardial infarction (Q waves, no acute changes).
commonMistakes: reading old Q waves as an acute event
steps.idealAnswer: Old inferior MI: established pathologic Q waves in II/III/aVF without acute ST elevation.

criticalActions: Evidence of prior inferior myocardial infarction (Q waves, no acute changes).
coreActions: pathologic Q waves in the inferior leads
recognizes these as old, not acute

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = small dense calcified granuloma (benign, old healed granulomatous disease). CREDIT: identifies the calcified granuloma (6); recognizes it as a benign healed finding, not malignancy (4). PENALTY: working up a clearly calcified benign nodule, or calling the study normal / missing the finding (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes it as a benign healed finding
commonMistakes: working up a clearly calcified benign nodule as malignancy
steps.idealAnswer: Calcified granuloma — a benign sequela of old granulomatous infection; dense uniform calcification.

coreActions: recognizes it as a benign healed finding

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Elevated acetaminophen level with rising AST/ALT and a climbing INR. What is your diagnosis and what predicts a poor outcome?
steps.rubric: Acetaminophen-induced acute liver injury / failure (6). Names poor-prognosis markers — rising INR, acidosis, renal failure, encephalopathy (King's College criteria) (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Acetaminophen-induced acute liver injury/failure. Poor-prognosis markers (King's College criteria): rising INR/coagulopathy, metabolic acidosis (low pH/high lactate), renal failure, and encephalopathy.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH acute liver failure (APAP): N-acetylcysteine, give empirically for toxic ingestion / deranged LFTs (CANT-MISS,8); activated charcoal if ingestion <4 h (3); plot timed level on Rumack-Matthew nomogram (3); monitor INR/pH/Cr/lactate/mental status – King's College criteria (4); hepatology/transplant referral for ALF (3). PENALTY: delaying NAC awaiting nomogram in a late/large ingestion (-4); missing transplant criteria (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: N-acetylcysteine is the antidote – give without delay for toxic ingestion/rising level or deranged transaminases (do not wait for nomogram if presentation is late or amount large). Activated charcoal if ingestion <4 h. Use the Rumack-Matthew nomogram for timed single ingestions. Monitor INR, pH, creatinine, lactate, and mental status; apply King's College criteria – contact hepatology/transplant for acute liver failure.

coreActions.item: Monitor INR/pH/Cr/lactate and mental status (King's College)

optionalActions: Monitor INR/pH/Cr/lactate and mental status (King's College)

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 25F presents with nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant pain; she has been taking large amounts of an over-the-counter pain reliever for several days. The diagnosis is Acetaminophen-induced liver injury, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: RIGHT upper quadrant tenderness over the liver; no rebound.
Abdomen: RIGHT upper quadrant tenderness over the liver; no rebound.
Neuro: alert; check for asterixis/encephalopathy (a late, ominous sign) if examined.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acetaminophen-induced liver injury while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acetaminophen-induced liver injury and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalWorkupItems: Serum ACETAMINOPHEN level with timing of ingestion
criticalInterpretationItems: Evidence of prior inferior myocardial infarction (Q waves, no acute changes).
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Evidence of prior inferior myocardial infarction (Q waves, no acute changes).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Evidence of prior inferior myocardial infarction (Q waves, no acute changes).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
